```asp
% -------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   For each example there is exactly one colour C such that:
%     * all cells of colour C form a rectangular block,
%     * the block is completely filled (no holes),
%     * its bounding box encloses every C‑cell and no other colour.
%   The output grid is the rectangle of that colour, starting at (0,0)
%   with size Height(C) × Width(C).  All other cells are omitted.
% -------------------------------------------------------------

% Helper predicate: a cell (R,Col) has colour C
row_of_color(C,R) :- input(R,Col,C).

% Row extremes for each colour
max_row(C, MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : row_of_color(C,R) }.
min_row(C, MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : row_of_color(C,R) }.

% Number of occurrences of a colour
count_c(C, Count) :- Count = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% Height (row span) of the minimal bounding box of colour C
height(C, Height) :- max_row(C, MaxR), min_row(C, MinR), Height = MaxR - MinR + 1.

% Width (column span) derived from count / height
width(C, Width) :- count_c(C, Count), height(C, Height), Width = Count / Height.

% Area of the rectangle for colour C
area(C, Area) :- height(C, Height), width(C, Width), Area = Height * Width.

% Full rectangle: the rectangle is completely filled with colour C
full(C) :- count_c(C, Count), area(C, Area), Count = Area.

% Exactly one colour forms a full rectangle
:- #count { C : full(C) } = 1.

% Helper for rectangle dimensions (used later)
full_dim(Col, Height, Width) :-
    full(Col),
    max_row(Col, MaxR),
    min_row(Col, MinR),
    Height = MaxR - MinR + 1,
    count_c(Col, Count),
    Width = Count / Height.

% Output cell (relative coordinates) for the unique full colour
output_cell(R, C, Col, Height, Width) :-
    full_dim(Col, Height, Width),
    R = 0..Height-1,
    C = 0..Width-1.

% No non‑full colour may be output
:- #count { Col : output_cell(R, C, Col, Height, Width), not full(Col) } = 0.

% Exactly one colour is used in the output
:- #count { Col : output_cell(R, C, Col, Height, Width), color(Col) } = 1.

% -------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   For each example there is exactly one colour C such that:
%     * all cells of colour C form a rectangular block,
%     * the block is completely filled (no holes),
%     * its bounding box encloses every C‑cell and no other colour.
%   The output grid is the rectangle of that colour, starting at (0,0)
%   with size Height(C) × Width(C).  All other cells are omitted.
%   The program therefore:
%     1. finds the unique colour C whose cells satisfy “full(C)”.
%     2. computes Height(C) = maxRow – minRow + 1 and Width(C) = count/Height.
%     3. generates the output cells inside the rectangle with that colour.
%   Uniqueness is enforced by:
%     – exactly one colour is “full”,
%     – the colour used in the output must be that full colour,
%     – exactly one colour appears in the output.
% -------------------------------------------------------------

% Helper predicate: a cell (R,Col) has colour C
row_of_color(C,R) :- input(R,Col,C).

% Row extremes for each colour
max_row(C, MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : row_of_color(C,R) }.
min_row(C, MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : row_of_color(C,R) }.

% Number of occurrences of a colour
count_c(C, Count) :- Count = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% Height (row span) of the minimal bounding box of colour C
height(C, Height) :- max_row(C, MaxR), min_row(C, MinR), Height = MaxR - MinR + 1.

% Width (column span) derived from count / height
width(C, Width) :- count_c(C, Count), height(C, Height), Width = Count / Height.

% Area of the rectangle for colour C
area(C, Area) :- height(C, Height), width(C, Width), Area = Height * Width.

% Full rectangle: the rectangle is completely filled with colour C
full(C) :- count_c(C, Count), area(C, Area), Count = Area.

% Exactly one colour forms a full rectangle
cntFull = #count { C : full(C) }.
:- cntFull = 1.

% Output‑cell (relative coordinates inside the rectangle of the (unique) full colour)
output_cell(R, C) :- full(Col), height(Col, Height), width(Col, Width),
                   R = X, C = Y, X = 0..Height-1, Y = 0..Width-1.

% Output cells with the colour of the rectangle
output(R, C, V) :- output_cell(R, C), full(V).

% Which colour actually appears in the output?
used(V) :- output(R, C, V), output_cell(R, C).

% No colour that is used may be non‑full
unusedFullCount = #count { V : used(V), not full(V) }.
:- unusedFullCount = 0.

% Exactly one colour is used in the output
usedCount = #count { V : used(V), color(V) }.
:- usedCount = 1.
rounds.program_after:
% -------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   For each example there is exactly one colour C such that:
%     * all cells of colour C form a rectangular block,
%     * the block is completely filled (no holes),
%     * its bounding box encloses every C‑cell and no other colour.
%   The output grid is the rectangle of that colour, starting at (0,0)
%   with size Height(C) × Width(C).  All other cells are omitted.
% -------------------------------------------------------------

% Helper predicate: a cell (R,Col) has colour C
row_of_color(C,R) :- input(R,Col,C).

% Row extremes for each colour
max_row(C, MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : row_of_color(C,R) }.
min_row(C, MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : row_of_color(C,R) }.

% Number of occurrences of a colour
count_c(C, Count) :- Count = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% Height (row span) of the minimal bounding box of colour C
height(C, Height) :- max_row(C, MaxR), min_row(C, MinR), Height = MaxR - MinR + 1.

% Width (column span) derived from count / height
width(C, Width) :- count_c(C, Count), height(C, Height), Width = Count / Height.

% Area of the rectangle for colour C
area(C, Area) :- height(C, Height), width(C, Width), Area = Height * Width.

% Full rectangle: the rectangle is completely filled with colour C
full(C) :- count_c(C, Count), area(C, Area), Count = Area.

% Exactly one colour forms a full rectangle
:- #count { C : full(C) } = 1.

% Helper for rectangle dimensions (used later)
full_dim(Col, Height, Width) :-
    full(Col),
    max_row(Col, MaxR),
    min_row(Col, MinR),
    Height = MaxR - MinR + 1,
    count_c(Col, Count),
    Width = Count / Height.

% Output cell (relative coordinates) for the unique full colour
output_cell(R, C, Col, Height, Width) :-
    full_dim(Col, Height, Width),
    R = 0..Height-1,
    C = 0..Width-1.

% No non‑full colour may be output
:- #count { Col : output_cell(R, C, Col, Height, Width), not full(Col) } = 0.

% Exactly one colour is used in the output
:- #count { Col : output_cell(R, C, Col, Height, Width), color(Col) } = 1.

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% -------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   For each example there is exactly one colour C such that:
%     * all cells of colour C form a rectangular block,
%     * the block is completely filled (no holes),
%     * its bounding box encloses every C‑cell and no other colour.
%   The output grid is the rectangle of that colour, starting at (0,0)
%   with size Height(C) × Width(C).  All other cells are omitted.
% -------------------------------------------------------------

% Helper predicate: a cell (R,Col) has colour C
row_of_color(C,R) :- input(R,Col,C).

% Row extremes for each colour (safe bindings)
max_row(C, MaxR) :- input(_,_,C), MaxR = #max { R : row_of_color(C,R) }.
min_row(C, MinR) :- input(_,_,C), MinR = #min { R : row_of_color(C,R) }.

% Count occurrences of a colour (safe binding)
count_c(C, Count) :- input(_,_,C), Count = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% Height (row span) of the minimal bounding box of colour C
height(C, Height) :- max_row(C, MaxR), min_row(C, MinR), Height = MaxR - MinR + 1.

% Width (column span) derived from count / height
width(C, Width) :- count_c(C, Count), height(C, Height), Width = Count / Height.

% Area of the rectangle for colour C
area(C, Area) :- height(C, Height), width(C, Width), Area = Height * Width.

% Full rectangle: the rectangle is completely filled with colour C
full(C) :- count_c(C, Count), area(C, Area), Count = Area.

% Exactly one colour forms a full rectangle
:- #count { C : full(C) } = 1.

% Helper for rectangle dimensions (used later)
full_dim(Col, Height, Width) :-
    full(Col),
    max_row(Col, MaxR),
    min_row(Col, MinR),
    Height = MaxR - MinR + 1,
    count_c(Col, Count),
    Width = Count / Height.

% Output cell (relative coordinates) for the unique full colour
output_cell(R, C, Col, Height, Width) :-
    full_dim(Col, Height, Width),
    R = 0..Height-1,
    C = 0..Width-1.

% No non‑full colour may be output
:- #count { Col : output_cell(R, C, Col, Height, Width), not full(Col) } = 0.

% Define colour predicate (used in the final count)
color(C) :- full(C).

% Exactly one colour is used in the output
:- #count { Col : output_cell(R, C, Col, Height, Width), color(Col) } = 1.

% -------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   For each example there is exactly one colour C such that:
%     * all cells of colour C form a rectangular block,
%     * the block is completely filled (no holes),
%     * its bounding box encloses every C‑cell and no other colour.
%   The output grid is the rectangle of that colour, starting at (0,0)
%   with size Height(C) × Width(C).  All other cells are omitted.
% -------------------------------------------------------------

% Helper predicate: a cell (R,Col) has colour C
row_of_color(C,R) :- input(R,Col,C).

% Row extremes for each colour
max_row(C, MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : row_of_color(C,R) }.
min_row(C, MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : row_of_color(C,R) }.

% Number of occurrences of a colour
count_c(C, Count) :- Count = #count { R,Col : input(R,Col,C) }.

% Height (row span) of the minimal bounding box of colour C
height(C, Height) :- max_row(C, MaxR), min_row(C, MinR), Height = MaxR - MinR + 1.

% Width (column span) derived from count / height
width(C, Width) :- count_c(C, Count), height(C, Height), Width = Count / Height.

% Area of the rectangle for colour C
area(C, Area) :- height(C, Height), width(C, Width), Area = Height * Width.

% Full rectangle: the rectangle is completely filled with colour C
full(C) :- count_c(C, Count), area(C, Area), Count = Area.

% Exactly one colour forms a full rectangle
:- #count { C : full(C) } = 1.

% Helper for rectangle dimensions (used later)
full_dim(Col, Height, Width) :-
    full(Col),
    max_row(Col, MaxR),
    min_row(Col, MinR),
    Height = MaxR - MinR + 1,
    count_c(Col, Count),
    Width = Count / Height.

% Output cell (relative coordinates) for the unique full colour
output_cell(R, C, Col, Height, Width) :-
    full_dim(Col, Height, Width),
    R = 0..Height-1,
    C = 0..Width-1.

% No non‑full colour may be output
:- #count { Col : output_cell(R, C, Col, Height, Width), not full(Col) } = 0.

% Exactly one colour is used in the output
:- #count { Col : output_cell(R, C, Col, Height, Width), color(Col) } = 1.

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

% Transformation rule: 
% Identify the smallest maximal uniform‑color rectangle (a solid block of a single colour that cannot be enlarged while keeping the colour). 
% This rectangle is moved to the top‑left corner of the grid, keeping its shape and colour. 
% All other cells are background (0) – we only output the cells of the rectangle, coloured with the colour that defined the rectangle. 
% The ASP encoding enumerates all maximal uniform‑colour rectangles, selects the one with the smallest area, and then outputs exactly the cells of that rectangle placed at (0,0). 
% Constraints enforce that no other colour appears inside the rectangle, that exactly one rectangle of minimal area exists, and that each output cell is coloured correctly.

% Determine grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Define rectangle candidates with uniform colour and maximal
candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    R1 = 0..H-1,
    R2 = R1..H-1,
    C1 = 0..W-1,
    C2 = C1..W-1,
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% Uniform colour: no cell inside the rectangle has a different colour
bad(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    input(R,C,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V.
uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- not bad(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% Side‑blocking (maximal) – each side must be adjacent to a different colour or the border
% up
count_up(R1,C1,C2,V,Count) :- Count = #count { C : input(R1-1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V }.
up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V) :- R1 = 0.
up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V) :- count_up(R1,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

% down
count_down(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- Count = #count { C : input(R2+1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V }.
down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- R2 = H-1.
down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- count_down(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

% left
count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- Count = #count { R : input(R,C1-1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V }.
left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- C1 = 0.
left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

% right
count_right(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- Count = #count { R : input(R,C2+1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V }.
right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- C2 = W-1.
right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- count_right(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V),
    down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% Area of a rectangle
area(R1,R2,C1,C2,Area) :- Area = (R2 - R1 + 1)*(C2 - C1 + 1).

% Enumerate all rectangles with a colour and compute their area
rect_area(MinA,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), MinA = area(R1,R2,C1,C2).

% The smallest area among all candidate rectangles
MinArea = #min { A : rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.

% The selected rectangle (the one with minimal area)
best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), area(R1,R2,C1,C2,Area), Area = MinArea.

% Cells belonging to the selected rectangle (placed at the top‑left corner)
outHeight(R1,R2,Height) :- Height = R2 - R1.
outWidth(C1,C2,Width)  :- Width = C2 - C1.
cell(R,C) :- best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), R = 0..Height, C = 0..Width.

% Exactly one output colour per cell (choose the colour that belongs to the rectangle)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% No other colour may appear inside the rectangle
:- best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), output(R,C,Col), V != Col.

% Exactly one rectangle of minimal area exists
count_best := #count { R : best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.
:- count_best = 1.

% At most one colour per cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% Transformation rule:
% Identify the smallest maximal uniform‑colour rectangle.
% This rectangle (a solid block of a single colour that cannot be enlarged
% while preserving the colour) is moved to the top‑left corner of the grid,
% keeping its shape and colour.  All other cells become background (0).
% The program enumerates all maximal uniform‑colour rectangles, selects the
% one with the smallest area and outputs exactly its cells placed at (0,0).
% Constraints enforce uniqueness of the chosen rectangle, that no other
% colour appears inside it and that exactly one output per cell is produced.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

Now define height/width, etc.

We'll write:

% Grid size
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% --- helper predicates -------------------------------------------------

% Bad cell inside a rectangle (different colour)
bad(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    input(R,C,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V.

% Uniform colour (no bad cell)
uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- not bad(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% --- maximal side‑blocking ---------------------------------------------

% Up side
count_up(R1,C1,C2,V,Count) :- Count = #count { C : input(R1-1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V }.
up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V) :- R1 = 0.
up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V) :- count_up(R1,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

% Down side
count_down(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- Count = #count { C : input(R2+1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V }.
down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- R2 = H-1.
down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- count_down(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

% Left side
count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- Count = #count { R : input(R,C1-1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V }.
left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- C1 = 0.
left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

% Right side
count_right(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- Count = #count { R : input(R,C2+1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V }.
right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- C2 = W-1.
right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- count_right(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V),
    down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% --- candidate rectangles (uniform + maximal) ---------------------------

candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    R1 = 0..H-1,
    R2 = R1..H-1,
    C1 = 0..W-1,
    C2 = C1..W-1,
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% --- area of a rectangle ------------------------------------------------
area(R1,R2,C1,C2,Area) :- Area = (R2 - R1 + 1)*(C2 - C1 + 1).

% Enumerate area together with a candidate rectangle
rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), A = area(R1,R2,C1,C2).

% Minimal area among all candidates
MinArea = #min { A : rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.

% Selected rectangle (the one with the minimal area)
best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), area(R1,R2,C1,C2,Area), Area = MinArea.

% Cells belonging to the chosen rectangle (placed at the top‑left corner)
cell(R,C) :- best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), R = 0..(R2 - R1), C = 0..(C2 - C1).

% Choose exactly one colour for each cell
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% The colour of the output cell must be the rectangle's colour
:- best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), output(R,C,Col), V != Col.

% Uniqueness per cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% Exactly one rectangle of minimal area must be selected
count_best := #count { R : best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.
:- count_best = 1.

End.

% Determine grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Candidate rectangles
candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    R1 = 0..H-1,
    R2 = R1..H-1,
    C1 = 0..W-1,
    C2 = C1..W-1,
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% Uniform colour check
bad(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    input(R,C,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V.
uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- not bad(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% Side blocking predicates
count_up(R1,C1,C2,V,Count) :- Count = #count { C : input(R1-1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V }.
up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V) :- R1 = 0.
up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V) :- count_up(R1,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

count_down(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- Count = #count { C : input(R2+1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V }.
down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- R2 = H-1.
down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- count_down(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- Count = #count { R : input(R,C1-1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V }.
left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- C1 = 0.
left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

count_right(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- Count = #count { R : input(R,C2+1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V }.
right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- C2 = W-1.
right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- count_right(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V),
    down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),

% Transformation rule:
%   Identify the smallest *maximal* rectangle of a single colour.
%   A rectangle is maximal when it cannot be enlarged in any

%   or the border of the grid.  Among all such maximal uniform‑colour

% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------

height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------

down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- count_down(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- Count = #count { R : input(R,C1-1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V }.

left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.

```asp
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   Identify the smallest *maximal* rectangle of a single colour.
%   A rectangle is maximal when it cannot be enlarged in any
%   direction (up, down, left, right) without meeting a different colour
%   or the border of the grid.  Among all such maximal uniform‑colour
%   rectangles the one with the smallest area is selected and moved to
%   the top‑left corner (rows 0..h-1, cols 0..w-1).  Its colour stays the
%   same and the rest of the grid becomes background (colour 0).  The
%   constraints enforce that exactly this rectangle is chosen, that no
%   other colour appears inside it and that every output cell is coloured
%   correctly.  The harness supplies the predicates input/3 and colour/1.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Compute grid dimensions
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)  :- H = 1 + Rmax, max_row(Rmax).

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)   :- W = 1 + Cmax, max_col(Cmax).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Helper predicates
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% A cell inside the rectangle that has a different colour makes the rectangle non‑uniform.
bad(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    input(R,C,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V.
% Uniform colour = no “bad” cell.
uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- not bad(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Side‑blocking predicates (maximal rectangle)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Up side – either at the top border or at least one column above the rectangle has a different colour.
count_up(R1,C1,C2,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { C : input(R1-1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V }.
up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V) :- 
    R1 = 0.
up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V) :- 
    count_up(R1,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

% Down side.
count_down(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { C : input(R2+1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V }.
down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    R2 = H-1.
down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    count_down(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

% Left side.
count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { R : input(R,C1-1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V }.
left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    C1 = 0.
left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

% Right side.
count_right(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { R : input(R,C2+1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V }.
right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    C2 = W-1.
right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    count_right(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V),
    down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Candidate rectangles (uniform colour + maximal)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    R1 = 0..H-1,
    R2 = R1..H-1,
    C1 = 0..W-1,
    C2 = C1..W-1,
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Area of a rectangle
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    A = (R2 - R1 + 1)*(C2 - C1 + 1).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Minimal area among all maximal rectangles
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
min_area(MinA) :-
    MinA = #min { A : rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The chosen rectangle – the maximal uniform‑colour rectangle with the smallest area.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    Area = (R2 - R1 + 1)*(C2 - C1 + 1),
    min_area(MinA),
    Area = MinA.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Cells belonging to the chosen rectangle (placed at the top‑left corner).
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    R = 0..(R2 - R1),
    C = 0..(C2 - C1).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Output – exactly one colour for each cell.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Constraints
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The colour of the output cell must be the colour of the chosen rectangle.
:- best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), output(R,C,Col), V != Col.

% No two different colours on the same cell.
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% Exactly one maximal rectangle of minimal area exists.
bestCount(B) :-
    B = #count { R : best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.
:- bestCount(B), B = 1.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The program is ready.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
```

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   Identify the smallest *maximal* rectangle of a single colour.
%   A rectangle is maximal when it cannot be enlarged in any
%   direction (up, down, left, right) without meeting a different colour
%   or the border of the grid.  Among all such maximal uniform‑colour
%   rectangles the one with the smallest area is selected and moved to
%   the top‑left corner (rows 0..h-1, cols 0..w-1).  Its colour stays the
%   same and the rest of the grid becomes background (colour 0).  The
%   constraints enforce that exactly this rectangle is chosen, that no
%   other colour appears inside it and that every output cell is coloured
%   correctly.  The harness supplies the predicates input/3 and colour/1.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Compute grid dimensions
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)  :- H = 1 + Rmax, max_row(Rmax).

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)   :- W = 1 + Cmax, max_col(Cmax).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Helper predicates
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
bad(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    input(R,C,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V.

uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- not bad(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Side‑blocking predicates (maximal rectangle)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
count_up(R1,C1,C2,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { C : input(R1-1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V }.
up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V) :- 
    R1 = 0.
up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V) :- 
    count_up(R1,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

count_down(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { C : input(R2+1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V }.
down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    R2 = H-1.
down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    count_down(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { R : input(R,C1-1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V }.
left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    C1 = 0.
left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

count_right(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { R : input(R,C2+1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V }.
right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    C2 = W-1.
right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    count_right(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V),
    down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Candidate rectangles (uniform colour + maximal)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    R1 = 0..H-1,
    R2 = R1..H-1,
    C1 = 0..W-1,
    C2 = C1..W-1,
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Area of a rectangle
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    A = (R2 - R1 + 1)*(C2 - C1 + 1).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Minimal area among all maximal rectangles
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
min_area(MinA) :-
    MinA = #min { A : rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The chosen rectangle – the maximal uniform‑colour rectangle with the smallest area.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    Area = (R2 - R1 + 1)*(C2 - C1 + 1),
    min_area(MinA),
    Area = MinA.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Cells belonging to the chosen rectangle (placed at the top‑left corner).
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    R = 0..(R2 - R1),
    C = 0..(C2 - C1).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Output – exactly one colour for each cell.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Constraints
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The colour of the output cell must be the colour of the chosen rectangle.
:- best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), output(R,C,Col), V != Col.

% No two different colours on the same cell.
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% Exactly one maximal rectangle of minimal area exists.
bestCount(N) :-
    N = #count { R : best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.
:- bestCount(N), N = 1.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The program is ready.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Helper predicates
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% A cell inside the rectangle that has a different colour makes the rectangle non‑uniform.
bad(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    input(R,C,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V.
% Uniform colour = no “bad” cell.
uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- not bad(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Side‑blocking predicates (maximal rectangle)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Up side – either at the top border or at least one column above the rectangle has a different colour.
count_up(R1,C1,C2,V,Count) :- Count = #count { C : input(R1-1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V }.
up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V) :- R1 = 0.
up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V) :- count_up(R1,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

% Down side.
count_down(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- Count = #count { C : input(R2+1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V }.
down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- R2 = H-1.
down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- count_down(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

% Left side.
count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- Count = #count { R : input(R,C1-1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V }.
left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- C1 = 0.
left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

% Right side.
count_right(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- Count = #count { R : input(R,C2+1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V }.
right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- C2 = W-1.
right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- count_right(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V),
    down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Candidate rectangles (uniform colour + maximal)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    R1 = 0..H-1,
    R2 = R1..H-1,
    C1 = 0..W-1,
    C2 = C1..W-1,
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Area of a rectangle
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
area(R1,R2,C1,C2,Area) :- Area = (R2 - R1 + 1)*(C2 - C1 + 1).

% Associate each candidate rectangle with its area.
rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), A = area(R1,R2,C1,C2).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The minimal area among all maximal rectangles.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
MinArea = #min { A : rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The chosen rectangle – the maximal uniform‑colour rectangle with the smallest area.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), area(R1,R2,C1,C2,Area), Area = MinArea.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Cells belonging to the chosen rectangle (placed at the top‑left corner).
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :- best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), R = 0..(R2 - R1), C = 0..(C2 - C1).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Output – exactly one colour for each cell.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Constraints
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The colour of the output cell must be the colour of the chosen rectangle.
:- best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), output(R,C,Col), V != Col.

% No two different colours on the same cell.
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% Exactly one maximal rectangle of minimal area exists.
bestCount = #count { R : best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.
:- bestCount = 1.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The program is ready.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   Identify the smallest *maximal* rectangle of a single colour.
%   A rectangle is maximal when it cannot be enlarged in any
%   direction (up, down, left, right) without meeting a different colour
%   or the border of the grid.  Among all such maximal uniform‑colour
%   rectangles the one with the smallest area is selected and moved to
%   the top‑left corner (rows 0..h-1, cols 0..w-1).  Its colour stays the
%   same and the rest of the grid becomes background (colour 0).  The
%   constraints enforce that exactly this rectangle is chosen, that no
%   other colour appears inside it and that every output cell is coloured
%   correctly.  The harness supplies the predicates input/3 and colour/1.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Compute grid dimensions
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)  :- H = 1 + Rmax, max_row(Rmax).

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)   :- W = 1 + Cmax, max_col(Cmax).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Helper predicates
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
bad(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    input(R,C,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V.

uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- not bad(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Side‑blocking predicates (maximal rectangle)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
count_up(R1,C1,C2,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { C : input(R1-1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V }.
up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V) :- 
    R1 = 0.
up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V) :- 
    count_up(R1,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

count_down(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { C : input(R2+1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V }.
down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    R2 = H-1.
down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    count_down(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { R : input(R,C1-1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V }.
left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    C1 = 0.
left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

count_right(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { R : input(R,C2+1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V }.
right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    C2 = W-1.
right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    count_right(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V),
    down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Candidate rectangles (uniform colour + maximal)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    R1 = 0..H-1,
    R2 = R1..H-1,
    C1 = 0..W-1,
    C2 = C1..W-1,
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Area of a rectangle
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    A = (R2 - R1 + 1)*(C2 - C1 + 1).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Minimal area among all maximal rectangles
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
min_area(MinA) :-
    MinA = #min { A : rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The chosen rectangle – the maximal uniform‑colour rectangle with the smallest area.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    Area = (R2 - R1 + 1)*(C2 - C1 + 1),
    min_area(MinA),
    Area = MinA.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Cells belonging to the chosen rectangle (placed at the top‑left corner).
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    R = 0..(R2 - R1),
    C = 0..(C2 - C1).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Output – exactly one colour for each cell.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Constraints
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The colour of the output cell must be the colour of the chosen rectangle.
:- best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), output(R,C,Col), V != Col.

% No two different colours on the same cell.
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% Exactly one maximal rectangle of minimal area exists.
bestCount(N) :-
    N = #count { R : best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.
:- bestCount(N), N = 1.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The program is ready.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
```

```asp
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   Identify the smallest *maximal* rectangle of a single colour.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Compute grid dimensions
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)  :- H = 1 + Rmax, max_row(Rmax).

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)   :- W = 1 + Cmax, max_col(Cmax).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Helper predicates
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Uniform colour rectangle: no cell inside rectangle has a colour different from V.
uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    #count { R,C,Wc : input(R,C,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V } = 0.

% Maximal rectangle: cannot be enlarged in any direction.
maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    #count { C : input(R1-1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V } = 0,
    #count { C : input(R2+1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V } = 0,
    #count { R : input(R,C1-1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V } = 0,
    #count { R : input(R,C2+1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V } = 0.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Candidate rectangles (uniform colour + maximal)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    R1 = 0..H-1,
    R2 = R1..H-1,
    C1 = 0..W-1,
    C2 = C1..W-1.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Area of a rectangle
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    A = (R2 - R1 + 1) * (C2 - C1 + 1).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Minimal area among all maximal rectangles
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
min_area(MinA) :-
    MinA = #min { A : rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The chosen rectangle – the maximal uniform‑colour rectangle with the smallest area.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    Area = (R2 - R1 + 1) * (C2 - C1 + 1),
    min_area(MinA),
    Area = MinA.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Cells belonging to the chosen rectangle (placed at the top‑left corner).
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    R = 0..(R2 - R1),
    C = 0..(C2 - C1).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Output – exactly one colour for each cell.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Constraints
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The colour of the output cell must be the colour of the chosen rectangle.
:- best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), output(R,C,Col), V != Col.

% No two different colours on the same cell.
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% Exactly one minimal‑area maximal rectangle exists.
1 { best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) } 1 :- true.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The program is ready.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
```

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   Identify the smallest *maximal* rectangle of a single colour.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Compute grid dimensions
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)  :- H = 1 + Rmax, max_row(Rmax).

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)   :- W = 1 + Cmax, max_col(Cmax).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Helper predicates
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Uniform colour rectangle: no cell inside rectangle has a colour different from V.
uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    N = #count { R,C,Wc : input(R,C,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V },
    N = 0.

% Maximal rectangle: cannot be enlarged in any direction.
maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    CountUp = #count { C : input(R1-1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V },
    CountUp = 0,
    CountDown = #count { C : input(R2+1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V },
    CountDown = 0,
    CountLeft = #count { R : input(R,C1-1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V },
    CountLeft = 0,
    CountRight = #count { R : input(R,C2+1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V },
    CountRight = 0.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Candidate rectangles (uniform colour + maximal)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    R1 = 0..H-1,
    R2 = R1..H-1,
    C1 = 0..W-1,
    C2 = C1..W-1.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Area of a rectangle
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    A = (R2 - R1 + 1) * (C2 - C1 + 1).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Minimal area among all maximal rectangles
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
min_area(MinA) :-
    MinA = #min { A : rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The chosen rectangle – the maximal uniform‑colour rectangle with the smallest area.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    Area = (R2 - R1 + 1) * (C2 - C1 + 1),
    min_area(MinA),
    Area = MinA.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Cells belonging to the chosen rectangle (placed at the top‑left corner).
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    R = 0..(R2 - R1),
    C = 0..(C2 - C1).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Output – exactly one colour for each cell.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Constraints
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The colour of the output cell must be the colour of the chosen rectangle.
:- best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), output(R,C,Col), V != Col.

% No two different colours on the same cell.
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% Exactly one minimal‑area maximal rectangle exists.
1 { best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) } 1 :- true.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The program is ready.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
```

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   Identify the smallest *maximal* rectangle of a single colour.
%   A rectangle is maximal when it cannot be enlarged in any
%   direction (up, down, left, right) without meeting a different colour
%   or the border of the grid.  Among all such maximal uniform‑colour
%   rectangles the one with the smallest area is selected and moved to
%   the top‑left corner (rows 0..h-1, cols 0..w-1).  Its colour stays the
%   same and the rest of the grid becomes background (colour 0).  The
%   constraints enforce that exactly this rectangle is chosen, that no
%   other colour appears inside it and that every output cell is coloured
%   correctly.  The harness supplies the predicates input/3 and colour/1.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Compute grid dimensions
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)  :- H = 1 + Rmax, max_row(Rmax).

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)   :- W = 1 + Cmax, max_col(Cmax).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Helper predicates
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
bad(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    input(R,C,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V.

uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- not bad(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Side‑blocking predicates (maximal rectangle)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
count_up(R1,C1,C2,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { C : input(R1-1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V }.
up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V) :- 
    R1 = 0.
up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V) :- 
    count_up(R1,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

count_down(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { C : input(R2+1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V }.
down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    R2 = H-1.
down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    count_down(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { R : input(R,C1-1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V }.
left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    C1 = 0.
left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    count_left(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

count_right(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count) :- 
    Count = #count { R : input(R,C2+1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V }.
right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    C2 = W-1.
right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- 
    count_right(R1,R2,C1,C2,V,Count), Count >= 1.

maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    up_blocked(R1,C1,C2,V),
    down_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    left_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    right_blocked(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Candidate rectangles (uniform colour + maximal)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    R1 = 0..H-1,
    R2 = R1..H-1,
    C1 = 0..W-1,
    C2 = C1..W-1,
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Area of a rectangle
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    A = (R2 - R1 + 1)*(C2 - C1 + 1).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Minimal area among all maximal rectangles
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
min_area(MinA) :-
    MinA = #min { A : rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The chosen rectangle – the maximal uniform‑colour rectangle with the smallest area.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    Area = (R2 - R1 + 1)*(C2 - C1 + 1),
    min_area(MinA),
    Area = MinA.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Cells belonging to the chosen rectangle (placed at the top‑left corner).
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    R = 0..(R2 - R1),
    C = 0..(C2 - C1).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Output – exactly one colour for each cell.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Constraints
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The colour of the output cell must be the colour of the chosen rectangle.
:- best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), output(R,C,Col), V != Col.

% No two different colours on the same cell.
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% Exactly one maximal rectangle of minimal area exists.
bestCount(N) :-
    N = #count { R : best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.
:- bestCount(N), N = 1.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The program is ready.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   Identify the smallest *maximal* rectangle of a single colour.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Compute grid dimensions
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)  :- H = 1 + Rmax, max_row(Rmax).

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)   :- W = 1 + Cmax, max_col(Cmax).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Helper predicates
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Uniform colour rectangle: no cell inside rectangle has a colour different from V.
uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    N = #count { R,C,Wc : input(R,C,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V },
    N = 0.

% Maximal rectangle: cannot be enlarged in any direction.
maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    CountUp = #count { C : input(R1-1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V },
    CountUp = 0,
    CountDown = #count { C : input(R2+1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V },
    CountDown = 0,
    CountLeft = #count { R : input(R,C1-1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V },
    CountLeft = 0,
    CountRight = #count { R : input(R,C2+1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V },
    CountRight = 0.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Candidate rectangles (uniform colour + maximal)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    R1 = 0..H-1,
    R2 = R1..H-1,
    C1 = 0..W-1,
    C2 = C1..W-1.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Area of a rectangle
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    A = (R2 - R1 + 1) * (C2 - C1 + 1).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Minimal area among all maximal rectangles
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
min_area(MinA) :-
    MinA = #min { A : rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The chosen rectangle – the maximal uniform‑colour rectangle with the smallest area.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    Area = (R2 - R1 + 1) * (C2 - C1 + 1),
    min_area(MinA),
    Area = MinA.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Cells belonging to the chosen rectangle (placed at the top‑left corner).
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    R = 0..(R2 - R1),
    C = 0..(C2 - C1).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Output – exactly one colour for each cell.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Constraints
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The colour of the output cell must be the colour of the chosen rectangle.
:- best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), output(R,C,Col), V != Col.

% No two different colours on the same cell.
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% Exactly one minimal‑area maximal rectangle exists.
1 { best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) } 1 :- true.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% The program is ready.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
```

```
% Compute grid dimensions
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H) :- H = 1 + Rmax, max_row(Rmax).

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + Cmax, max_col(Cmax).

% Helper predicate to make variables safe
safe(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :- true.
safe_grid(H,W) :- true.

% Uniform colour rectangle: no cell inside rectangle has a colour different from V.
uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    N = #count { R,C,Wc : input(R,C,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V },
    N = 0,
    safe(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% Maximal rectangle: cannot be enlarged in any direction.
maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    CountUp = #count { C : input(R1-1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V },
    CountUp = 0,
    CountDown = #count { C : input(R2+1,C,Wc), C1 <= C, C <= C2, Wc != V },
    CountDown = 0,
    CountLeft = #count { R : input(R,C1-1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V },
    CountLeft = 0,
    CountRight = #count { R : input(R,C2+1,Wc), R1 <= R, R <= R2, Wc != V },
    CountRight = 0.

% Candidate rectangles (uniform colour + maximal)
candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    safe_grid(H,W),
    R1 = 0..H-1,
    R2 = R1..H-1,
    C1 = 0..W-1,
    C2 = C1..W-1.

% Area of a rectangle
rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    A = (R2 - R1 + 1) * (C2 - C1 + 1).

% Minimal area among all maximal rectangles
min_area(MinA) :-
    MinA = #min { A : rect_area(A,R1,R2,C1,C2,V) }.

% The chosen rectangle – the maximal uniform‑colour rectangle with the smallest area.
best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) :-
    candidate(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    uniform(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    maximal(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    Area = (R2 - R1 + 1) * (C2 - C1 + 1),
    min_area(MinA),
    Area = MinA,
    safe(R1,R2,C1,C2,V).

% Cells belonging to the chosen rectangle (placed at the top‑left corner).
cell(R,C) :-
    best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V),
    R = 0..(R2 - R1),
    C = 0..(C2 - C1).

% Output – exactly one colour for each cell.
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% Constraints
:- best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V), output(R,C,Col), V != Col.
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
:- 1 { best(R1,R2,C1,C2,V) } 1 :- true.
```
